ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori-spiced cauliflower; closest parent 'cauliflower food product' (FOODON_03311737) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Cauliflower) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for tandoori-marinated cauliflower preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for tandoori cauliflower dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Gobi Tandoori is approximately 200 g, comprising several florets of marinated and tandoor-roasted cauliflower served with mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Cauliflower, cooked; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for cauliflower roasted with spices
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base cauliflower cooked values from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (cooked cauliflower). Adjustments for tandoori marinade (yogurt, oil, spices) applied using USDA FoodData Central reference data for roasted spiced cauliflower (FDC ID 2346415 and analogous entries). Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate recalculated to reflect marinated and roasted preparation. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Cauliflower, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gobi Tandoori. GI of 15 is based on the closest analogous food: cooked cauliflower, GI ≈ 15 (low), as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Tandoori marinade (yogurt, spices, small amount of oil) does not materially alter the GI of the cauliflower base. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 9.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.85, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tandoori marinade and spice blend
hypertension.guidance: Gobi Tandoori contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g, which can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium intake; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of under 3 per standard serving, Gobi Tandoori is a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the small amount of yogurt and oil in the marinade should be accounted for in total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Gobi Tandoori is low in saturated fat (1.1 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre (2.8 g per 100 g) along with anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and cumin, making it a heart-friendly vegetable dish when prepared with minimal added oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gobi Tandoori is an Indian vegetarian dish consisting of cauliflower florets marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically incorporating tandoori masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chili powder, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then roasted at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven or a modern oven. The result is cauliflower with a lightly charred, smoky exterior and a tender interior, carrying the complex warmth of Indian spice blends. The dish is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Indian restaurants across Singapore and the Indian subcontinent, often accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, Gobi Tandoori is a low-calorie, low-glycemic vegetable dish providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (3.8 g), modest fat (5.2 g, predominantly unsaturated), and low carbohydrate (9.5 g). It is a good source of dietary fibre (2.8 g per 100 g), vitamin C (28 mg), folate (44 mcg DFE), and potassium (320 mg). The sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal. Its low glycemic index (approximately 15) makes it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and cumin contribute to its cardiovascular-friendly profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Gobi Tandoori?
Is Gobi Tandoori good for diabetics?
What spices are used in Gobi Tandoori?
What is the difference between Gobi Tandoori and Gobi Tikka?
Is Gobi Tandoori high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Gobi Tandoori exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a finished dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked cauliflower data supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for roasted spiced cauliflower, with adjustments for the tandoori marinade. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked cauliflower) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates for the finished Gobi Tandoori dish per 100 g edible portion, adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked cauliflower and USDA FoodData Central roasted spiced cauliflower data. Exact restaurant formulations will vary. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source data for this preparation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats in the standard preparation. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked cauliflower from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Gobi Tandoori was found in approved sources. 5. The input description contained a typographical error ('Coasted' instead of 'Coated'); corrected to 'Cauliflower florets marinated in spiced yogurt and roasted in a tandoor oven.' 6. This record was sourced from field survey data (1 restaurant: Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry - Boat Quay); field survey is not used as a nutritional source but is preserved as provenance context for the food identity.
